examples.title: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange Implementation
examples.description: Implementation showing parameter generation, key computation, and shared secret derivation
examples.code:
# Step 1: Generate DH Parameters (typically done once)
FUNCTION generate_dh_parameters(security_bits=2048):
    # Select a safe prime p = 2q + 1 where q is also prime
    p = generate_safe_prime(security_bits)
    q = (p - 1) // 2
    
    # Select a generator g of the subgroup of order q
    g = select_generator(p, q)
    
    RETURN (p, g)

# Step 2: Generate Private and Public Keys
FUNCTION generate_key_pair(p, g):
    # Private key: random integer in [1, p-2]
    private_key = random_integer(2, p-2)
    
    # Public key: g^private_key mod p
    public_key = pow(g, private_key, p)
    
    RETURN (private_key, public_key)

# Step 3: Compute Shared Secret
FUNCTION compute_shared_secret(their_public_key, my_private_key, p):
    shared_secret = pow(their_public_key, my_private_key, p)
    RETURN shared_secret

# Step 4: Key Derivation using HKDF
FUNCTION derive_keys(shared_secret, info, length=64):
    # Apply hash to strengthen and expand
    prk = HMAC-SHA256(shared_secret, b"DH key expansion")
    okm = HKDF-expand(prk, info, length)
    
    encryption_key = okm[0:32]  # 256-bit AES key
    mac_key = okm[32:64]        # 256-bit HMAC key
    
    RETURN (encryption_key, mac_key)
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the complete DH key exchange process including parameter generation using safe primes, key pair generation, shared secret computation using modular exponentiation, and key derivation using HKDF to produce cryptographic keys suitable for encryption and authentication.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log p) for modular exponentiation using square-and-multiply

examples.title: Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol
examples.description: Authenticated key exchange protocol with digital signatures providing MITM protection
examples.code:
# STS Protocol - Alice and Bob each have signing keys

# System Setup
CERT_A = certificate_of(Alice)  # Contains Alice's public key, signed by CA
CERT_B = certificate_of(Bob)

# Step 1: Exchange Public Values (same as basic DH)
Alice:
    a = random(2, p-2)
    g_a = pow(g, a, p)
    SEND(g_a, CERT_A) TO Bob

Bob:
    b = random(2, p-2)
    g_b = pow(g, b, p)
    SEND(g_b, CERT_B) TO Alice

# Step 2: Sign and Exchange (provides authentication)
Alice:
    VERIFY(CERT_B) using trusted_CA_public_key
    signature_A = SIGN(Alice_private_key, (g_a, g_b))
    SEND(signature_A) TO Bob
    
    # Compute shared secret after verification
    S = pow(g_b, a, p)

Bob:
    VERIFY(CERT_A) using trusted_CA_public_key
    signature_B = SIGN(Bob_private_key, (g_b, g_a))
    SEND(signature_B) TO Alice
    
    # Compute shared secret
    S = pow(g_a, b, p)

# Step 3: Each party verifies received signature
Alice:
    VERIFY(signature_B, Bob_public_key, (g_b, g_a))
    IF verification fails: ABORT

Bob:
    VERIFY(signature_A, Alice_public_key, (g_a, g_b))
    IF verification fails: ABORT
examples.explanation: STS extends basic DH with digital signatures. Each party signs the concatenation of both public values, proving they possess the private key corresponding to their certificate. An attacker (including MITM) cannot forge these signatures without the private key, thus preventing the attack.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log p) for DH operations + O(n) for signature verification

examples.title: PRNG Seeding from Key Exchange
examples.description: Using DH shared secret to seed a cryptographically secure PRNG
examples.code:
# Using DH shared secret to seed a PRNG for continuous encryption

FUNCTION establish_secure_channel(DH_parameters):
    # Complete DH key exchange
    (a, g_a) = generate_key_pair(p, g)
    SEND g_a TO Bob
    RECEIVE g_b FROM Bob
    
    shared_secret = pow(g_b, a, p)  # g^(ab) mod p
    
    # Key Derivation using HKDF
    (enc_key, mac_key) = derive_keys(shared_secret, b"secure channel")
    
    # Initialize PRNG with derived key
    prng = CTR_DRBG(entropy_input=enc_key, personalization_string=mac_key)
    
    RETURN prng

FUNCTION encrypt_message(prng, plaintext):
    # Generate keystream from PRNG
    keystream = prng.generate(len(plaintext))
    
    # Encrypt using XOR (Stream cipher mode)
    ciphertext = plaintext XOR keystream
    
    RETURN ciphertext

# Security Properties:
# 1. DH provides high-entropy seed (computationally infeasable to predict)
# 2. HKDF ensures no related-key attacks between derived keys
# 3. CTR_DRBG provides forward secrecy when reseeded for each session
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the critical connection between key exchange protocols and PRNGs. The DH shared secret serves as entropy input to a deterministic random bit generator (DRBG), which then produces the keystream for encryption. This ensures all session keys are unpredictable and cryptographically secure.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for PRNG generation where n is output length
